I. PROJECT CONTEXT AND DEVELOPMENT OBJECTIVES



 A. CONTEXT AT APPRAISAL

    Context
1.       Togo is one of the smaller countries in Africa, with an estimated population of 7.3 million and an annual
population growth of about 2.6 percent in 2017. While Togo enjoyed strong economic growth averaging over 5 percent
per year between 2012 and 2015, mainly fueled by strong public investments in infrastructure and good performance in
the agricultural sector, by 2016 the macroeconomic situation worsened, with augmented indebtedness and an increasing
fiscal deficit.

2.      Seventy-five percent of the Togolese population was below the age of 35 at the time of appraisal, 60 percent
below the age of 25 and 42 percent below the age of 15. Fifty-four percent of the working age population was 35 years
old or younger, and, given the annual population growth rate, it was projected that about 35,000 youth would enter the
job market in 2016-2017. The unemployment rate was generally quite low for all age groups, standing at 2.7 and 2.9
percent, respectively, for the groups 15-24 and 25-34 (QUIBB, 2015). The age group 15 to 34 represented 75 percent of
the unemployed (ERI-ESI, 2017).

3.       Yet the low unemployment rates masked the real challenges of underemployment,1 which stood at 43.4
percent for the age group 25-34 (QUIBB, 2015). Poorer and vulnerable youth did not meet the formal definition of
unemployed since they would often perform ad hoc work to earn a survival livelihood, at very low levels of productivity.
Poor and extremely poor individuals showed much greater levels of underemployment than the non-poor (respectively,
- 43.4 percent and 41.5 percent compared to 21.5 percent of non-poor) (QUIBB, 2015). In addition, youth would earn
significantly less than non-youth: 221,700 FCFA (US$358) per year on average for the 25-34 group compared to 250,100
FCFA (US$404) per year for the group 35-54 and 262,500 (US$424) for the 55-64 age group (QUIBB, 2015).

4.       The challenges of unemployment and underemployment were exacerbated by the informality dominating
the labor market. In 2017, 9 out of 10 workers were in informal employment (90.8 percent of the active population in
the rural areas and 87.2 percent in the urban context; ERI-ESI, 2017). This prevalence was even more marked for women,
whose informal employment rate stood at 96.2 percent in 2017, compared to 78.8 percent for men (ERI-ESI, 2017).
Women have faced particular challenges accessing the production factors necessary for profitable (formal)
entrepreneurship (including land and finance).2 Women’s access to credit was especially difficult because of the lack of
guarantees and the riskiness of the informal sector.3

5.      In 2012 and 2013, the Government of Togo (GoT) had adopted several strategic documents intended to
improve youth employment indicators, attesting to the prioritization of this issue in the overall socio-economic
development of the country. Documents such as the National Employment Policy (PNE, 2013 - 2017), National Youth
Policy (PNJ, 2014 - 2017), Strategy for Boosting Growth and Promoting Employment (SCAPE, 2013 - 2017), Youth
Employment Program (PEJ, 2013 - 2017) and the National Strategic Plan for Youth Employment (PSNEJ 2013 - 2017) all
laid out concrete policy measures intended to combat youth unemployment, underemployment and informal

 1 Underemployment can be described in three ways: i) involuntarily working part time (underemployment by hours); ii) working full-time but earning less than a
 minimal salary, such as the minimum wage or poverty line (underemployment by earnings); and iii) a mismatch between one’s job and one’s education, training
 and experience (underemployment by mismatch).
 2
   According to the 2013-2014 EDST survey, only 20% of women between the ages of 45 and 49 own land.
 3 According to the survey conducted by INSEED in 2015, women would tend to resort to microfinance, which would mainly finance cooperatives and cover micro-

 projects at very high interest rates.


                                                                                                                                                Page 5 of 65
      The World Bank
     Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project (P157036)



employment in the medium term by promoting youth entrepreneurship; strengthening employability through provision
of labor market adapted vocational training; improving education quality and access to finance; and enhancing
decentralization of services.4

6.       At the time of appraisal, a new Country Partnership Framework 5 (CPF) for Togo was under preparation, calling
for support to employment creation and improved incomes and safety nets for the most vulnerable. In this framework,
the World Bank Group’s proposed approach to promoting employment between 2017 and 2020 focused on
strengthening the business environment and boosting private sector productivity, as well as maximizing employment
opportunities through new operations that would target a particular demographic facing the greatest employment
challenges and incorporating training components in proposed activities. The Project was aligned with this approach by
incorporating in its design components establishing mechanisms (e.g., participation in training and mentoring, promotion
of savings, provision of grants) to support economic inclusion of youth through the creation of income generating
activities. The Project was also aligned with the then most recent World Bank Togo Interim Strategy Note (ISN)6 covering
fiscal year (FY)12-13, which had three main clusters: (i) deepening the economic recovery process and promoting
sustainable development; (ii) supporting economic governance and transparency; and (iii) addressing urgent poverty
reduction and social needs. The Project would contribute primarily to the third objective, and in particular to outcome
3.1 “Improved access of communities to basic social and local development services (including youth employment).” The
Project also reflected the emphasis on job creation which were critical elements in both the IDA 17 and 18
replenishments.

7.      By focusing its efforts on providing employment opportunities for the most vulnerable youth, the Project
would contribute to the Higher-Level Objectives highlighted within the CPF, which called for addressing the main
constraints the country was facing for accelerating economic growth and sustaining progress toward the goal of reducing
extreme poverty and enhancing youth resilience.




 4 Youth employment programs in Togo frequently have overlapping goals, target groups and/or activities, thus resulting in fragmentation of
 resources. The list below outlines a few of the largest public programs:
          (a) National Volunteer Agency of Togo (ANVT) – launched in 2011 to strengthen employability and promote civic engagement through
               voluntary internships in predominantly public institutions with a maximum duration of three years. It targets young graduates and
               first-time job seekers (18-35 years). They had a budget of approximately 4 billion FCFA (partially financed by UNDP) (2011-2013). In
               the first 2 years, the program touched 3,900 youth. ANVT has recently launched a program for out-of-school youth (Out-of-school
               Youth Program - JDS) which is being scaled up after a pilot phase of 582 beneficiaries.
          (b) Hiring and Employment Support Program (AIDE) – implemented since 2011 by the National Employment Agency (ANPE). The
               program subsidizes six-month long internships (with the ability to extend to 1 year) in private companies. With a total budget of 2
               billion FCFA, it subsidized 1,623 internship allowances between 2011-2013.
          (c) Youth Economic Initiative Support Fund (FAIEJ) – launched in 2007 to provide technical, institutional and financial support for youth
               entrepreneurs. FAIEJ guarantees loans and provides mentorship to orient youth towards prospective economic areas, in addition to
               a number of other services. Their overall budget is 1 billion FCFA, targeting 4000 youths per year.
          (d) Youth Vocational Employment Support Project (PAIPJA) – launched in March 2011. It aims to promote and encourage self-
               employment, particularly for young graduates of vocational training, by supporting and assisting in the consolidation of their skills.
               With a budget of 2.5 billion FCFA, between 2011-2013, 2050 youths received labor market integration kits and training on
               enterprise creation and management.
          (e) Community Development Support Program (PRADEB) – a five-year program launched in 2013. It contains a specific component on
               the promotion of youth employment. With a total budget of 6 billion FCFA, it has contributed to the creation of 3,500 jobs since its
               inception.

     Theory of Change (Results Chain)

8.       The PAD did not describe a Theory of Change (ToC) as it was not an operational requirement at the time.7 For
this Implementation Completion and Results Report (ICR), the Project’s ToC is constructed ex-post and has been
retrofitted from the Project’s Results Framework (RF).

9.      Figure 1 presents Project activities, outputs, short term outcomes, the PDO, and its (medium- and long-term)
expected impacts. Component 1 was designed to finance preparatory activities that were required before the tasks under
Component 2 could be implemented, while activities under capacity building for the Government (Component 3.1) and
project management (Component 4) had an overall impact on all outcomes.

Figure 1. Theory of Change for the Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project8




     Project Development Objectives (PDOs)

10.    The objective of the project was to provide access to income generating opportunities to targeted poor and
vulnerable youth in Togo.

      Key Expected Outcomes and Outcome Indicators

11.    The key expected outcome was to provide access to income generating opportunities to targeted poor and
vulnerable youth9 in Togo. The PDO indicators, as defined in the PAD, are presented below. The Project’s Results
Framework (RF) also contained 15 Intermediate Results Indicators (Annex 1).

(a) Direct project beneficiaries (number), of which female (percentage) (Corporate Indicator);
(b) Number of person-days of participation in community service sub-projects;
(c) Targeted youth initiating or expanding household enterprises with support from the project (number);
(d) Beneficiaries of components 1 and 2 under the national poverty line, living with a disability, or not having finished
    primary school (percentage);10
(e) Beneficiaries that feel project investments reflected their needs (percentage).

      Components

 12.      The Project was comprised of four components. Activities under these components were estimated at US$15
 million. Detailed project costs, including allocated versus actual at the time of closing, are presented in Annex 3.

 13.      Component 1 - Community Service and Training (US$8.5 million), aimed to give poor and vulnerable youth
 beneficiaries the chance to develop good work habits and gain civic values by participating in something of value to the
 community, while providing on-the-job as well as soft skills and micro-entrepreneurial training. The stipend paid to
 beneficiaries for their community service, while low, was meant to permit beneficiaries to address immediate constraints
 related to poverty in order to devote their mental “bandwidth” towards working, learning, and eventually launching an
 income-generating activity. Only those beneficiaries who completed the community service and micro-entrepreneurial
 training in addition to meeting the saving threshold would receive cash grants under component 2.

       •   Subcomponent 1.1, Community service sub-projects provided community service opportunities to targeted poor
           and vulnerable youth using a community-driven approach.11

       •   Subcomponent 1.2 on Life skills, micro-entrepreneurial training, and on-the-job technical training was intended
           to boost technical skills and knowledge as well as life and micro-entrepreneurial skills. Participants were
           compensated at the same stipend as a normal workday under the Community service subcomponent.12

 14.     Component 2 - Support for Income Generating Activities (US$2.5 million), was to support beneficiaries who
 successfully completed the activities under Component 1, including preparation of a satisfactory basic business plan.
 They were eligible to receive support to launch or enlarge an income generating activity, through: (a) a matching grant


 9 The Project adopted the definition of youth proposed by the African Union, e.g., youth is every person between the ages of 15
 and 35 years (see the African Union Charter adopted on July 2, 2006 in Banjul, Gambia, and available at
 https://au.int/sites/default/files/treaties/7789-treaty-0033_-_african_youth_charter_e.pdf).
 10 The indicator was revised during the second restructuring: please see Table 2.
 11 The community works projects would be managed by elected Village Development Committees (VDCs), which would also be

 trained to help identify potential beneficiaries based on criteria provided by the project.
 12 The training proposed is based on the Formation en Développement d‘Affaires (FODA) model developed by GIZ and the

 Government of Togo and customized for youth with low levels of education and those with handicaps to include i) discussions on
 entrepreneurial behaviors and identification of one’s strengths and weaknesses; ii) generation of ideas for income -generating
 activities; iii) a rudimentary market assessment; iv) an evaluation of inputs and equipment required; v) a basic financial analysis; and
 vi) bringing all of these elements together in a business plan with assistance from a facilitator. The model also includes training on
 financial literacy including using a bank account, calculating profit/loss, and keeping an inventory. The modules are delivered in local
 languages by Togolese facilitators.

to top-up the savings realized by beneficiaries during their participation in the community service subcomponent of the
project, in order to provide start-up capital for their business; and (b) mentoring to help youth address problems that
may confront them as they establish or scale up their business, market themselves, and identify opportunities, followed
by ongoing periodic follow-up of the income generating activity.

15.         Component 3 - Capacity Building (US$1.0 million), had two subcomponents:

       •    Subcomponent 3.1., Capacity building for implementation of youth employment programs, aimed to support the
            Government to design, strengthen and put into action key operational tools for implementing youth
            employment programs targeting the poor and vulnerable. Capacity building covered geographical targeting of
            programs; targeting poor and extremely poor households with youth through community-based targeting and a
            proxy means test (PMT); developing well-functioning Management Information System (MIS) and electronic
            payments systems; improving program evaluation; and ensuring a well-functioning grievance redress system.

       •    Subcomponent 3.2. Community capacity-building had the objective of empowering communities to take a key
            role in implementing project activities and promoting accountability and transparency through the Grassroots
            Management Training approach (GMT). The approach covered information and communication, community
            organization and dynamics, participatory poverty and needs assessment, participatory and operational planning
            of the sub-project, community procurement of inputs, financial management, participatory monitoring and
            evaluation, and servicing and maintenance of infrastructure.

16.     Component 4- Project Management (US$3.0 million) supported activities related to project management and
coordination through the designated Project Implementation Unit (PIU), including staff-related costs, equipment,
vehicles, fuel, office space, and communications costs, and operating costs related to the procurement, supervision,
auditing and evaluation of project activities.


B. SIGNIFICANT CHANGES DURING IMPLEMENTATION (IF APPLICABLE)
17.     The Project was restructured twice. The first restructuring (prior to effectiveness, November 2017)
addressed delays in the recruitment of two Safeguards specialists, with subsequent need to convert their
recruitment as a dated covenant three months after effectiveness rather than by the effectiveness date. The
second restructuring (March 2020) addressed the need for additional time to complete coaching/mentoring
activities (Component 1) and adjustments to the results framework. Details of the two restructurings are
presented in Table 1 below.

II. OUTCOME

 A. RELEVANCE OF PDOs

    Assessment of Relevance of PDOs and Rating
23.      Rating: High. The Project’s PDO was highly relevant at the time of appraisal and continued to be relevant at
completion. At appraisal, the Project’s objective and design were relevant to the Government’s development priorities.
By promoting economic inclusion of beneficiaries through the provision of support for the opening of a bank account at
local microfinance institutions and the establishment of income generating activities, the Project was aligned with the
relevant national strategies16 calling for the improvement of youth employability and self-employment capacities as well
as the promotion of youth access to finance. The Project was also in line with the Prime Minister’s June 29, 2015 statement
announcing economic and social inclusion among the new government’s top policy priorities. In addition, the
establishment of a quota to ensure women’s participation in the Project was consistent with the strategic axes identified
within the National Youth Policy (PNJ, 2014 - 2017) and the National Employment Policy (PNE, 2013 - 2017) calling for the
promotion of initiatives to address inequalities among young men and women in accessing means of production and the
adoption of microfinance strategies aimed to broaden women’s access to microcredits. At completion, the Project’s
objective was aligned with the priorities identified by the GoT, in particular, the Government’s Roadmap for Togo 2025
(Feuille de Route Gouvernementale 2025) launched in October 2020, which, in its second strategic objective, calls for the
dynamization of job creation by mobilizing the economic resources of the country. Also, the Project was aligned with the
Government’s actions to mitigate the effects of COVID-1917, in synergy with the approach proposed through the Novissi
platform18 providing cash transfers to informal sector workers and poor households who were negatively impacted by the
emergency health measures implemented by the government.

30.      Delivery of Grassroots Management Training (GMT) under Component 3 also contributed to the achievement
of the Project objective by empowering communities to select, plan and implement labor-intensive works that could
respond to community needs and would be aligned to the predominant economic activity in the area, enhancing in
turn access to income-generating opportunities at the village level. Capacity-building of community development
committees (CDC) (including modules on community dynamics, procurement of inputs, financial management of projects,
servicing and maintenance of created infrastructure, among others) in targeted villages30 led to engaging community
members in the decision-making process for the selection of relevant sub-projects based on identified community needs.
Realized assets include (116) rehabilitated or created rural roads, (40) water reservoirs to support vegetable gardening
and livestock activities, (20) reforestation works and community plantations, (6) community quarrying sub-project, (15)
community agriculture sub-projects, and (3) community sanitation sub-projects. Visits conducted during the ICR mission
confirm that created assets improved access to employment opportunities at the village level: e.g., water reservoirs allow
for the irrigation of dry land, providing opportunities for cultivation; the creation or rehabilitation of feeder roads
facilitates the transportation of produce to markets, enabling households to sell it, etc.31 Collected anecdotal evidence
also indicates a strong sense of ownership of realized public goods by community members, and their commitment to
work on maintenance after the end of the Project. In addition, community capacity building provided an important entry
point for women to participate in sub-projects: to ensure the participation of the target of 50% women beneficiaries,
village development committees prioritized sub-projects suitable to and appealing for women in the village, enhancing in
turn women’s access to relevant income-generating opportunities.

through a twofold pathway: a) ANADEB had to establish from scratch a delivery system for the intervention, including,

functioning and represent key elements towards the sustainability of the Project; b) by reinforcing the decentralized

  C. EFFICIENCY
       Assessment of Efficiency and Rating

33.     The efficiency analysis considered the resources invested relative to the project outcomes. This is primarily
measured via cost-benefit analysis (CBA) that yields estimates for the project’s social rate of return (SRR). The benefits
were divided into those associated with the LIPWs and those with the entrepreneurship support activities. The benefits
counted for the subprojects’ activities (i.e., labor-intensive public works, LIPWs) are based on the immediate expenditures
(portion of the LIPW earnings that were not saved by beneficiaries) and a portion of the operations costs of the community
project that did not go to labor/beneficiary payments (i.e., inputs and management costs). The benefits from the
entrepreneurship support activities include the future income gains. Only net benefits are considered for each type of
income gain by subtracting an approximate opportunity cost; in lieu of not having an impact evaluation, three
conservative opportunity cost (counterfactual) scenarios were considered in the analysis.

34.     The analysis also measured how the project design, by pairing a LIPWs and an entrepreneurship support
intervention as it did, led to greater efficiency than had they been implemented as separate interventions. The two main
channels considered through which combining components led to improved efficiency are (1) incentivizing beneficiaries
to only spend what is needed from their LIPWs payments and (2) having LIPWs act as a targeting mechanism for
beneficiaries who end up joining the entrepreneurship support component. The first channel refers to using matching
grants as incentive to make individual assessment on how much they need for immediate expenditures and avoids
‘overshooting’ on the support needed. This causes beneficiaries to organically shift program resources from the lower
return LIPW activities to the higher return entrepreneurship support activities. The second channel considered is the
beneficiary targeting enabled by LIPWs, which discouraged potential beneficiaries with high opportunity costs. Even
though this targeting effect was not explicitly taken into account, it was expected to drive the income results from the
entrepreneurship component.

35.      Overall, the Project’s Efficiency is rated Substantial. The Project results are positive and substantial with an
estimated SRR between 53 and 88 percent, with a middle scenario of 72 percent. This is largely tied to the sizable net
effect found for the entrepreneurship support activities on long-term income - relative to other successful
entrepreneurship projects and despite having conservative assumptions regarding beneficiary opportunity costs. As
expected, and consistent with the literature, the measurable long-term benefits from the LIPW were small and on par
with other evidence, for example an earlier study33 found that on average it takes US$3.5 to generate US$1 of income
benefits as compared to a range between US$3.30 and US$4.40, US$4.40 and US$5.72.34 Due to the significant income
effect, the entrepreneurship component had a rate of return of 128 percent with 97 and 150 percent for the most and
least conservative counterfactuals, respectively. The results showed that combining the LIPWs and entrepreneurship
activities in the way it was done increased the project’s return by 30 to 40 percent. This difference was associated with
the number of beneficiaries (assuming everyone received the same size grant) being 11,495 when previously
implemented together as opposed to a projected 6,963 beneficiaries when implemented separately. The details and
specific assumptions of the CBA are included in Annex 4.

Services Project (P157038) significantly lowered the administrative costs for both Projects, with a positive impact on
administrative efficiency. Both Social Protection and Jobs (SPJ)-Projects were designed, launched, and implemented in

 D. JUSTIFICATION OF OVERALL OUTCOME RATING
38.     The overall outcome of the Project is rated as Highly Satisfactory. The Project development
objective’s relevance was High, its efficacy was High, and efficiency was Substantial at the end of the Project,
as described in the corresponding sections above. On the implementation side, the Project successfully
reacted to changing circumstances and crises, including through the two restructurings. Disbursement was
slow during the first year of implementation when the implementing agency had to set up the project
implementation unit, but it picked up quickly in the second year, almost in line with disbursement projections,
and maintained a sustained disbursement pace, also through the extension accorded during the second
restructuring. The Project was 99.9 percent disbursed by the closing date of December 31st, 2021.

41.      Anecdotal evidence gathered through the ICR mission suggested that gender dynamics have positively changed
in households and villages that participated in the Project. Women’s participation in the program led to enhanced self-
confidence and increased respect within the household (as they are now perceived as breadwinners and less dependent

made through the Project, to their husband’s activities, gaining recognition and respect within the family. In addition,
female participation during village meetings to discuss sub-project activities strengthened their self-confidence vis-à-vis
the rest of the community, increasing their active contribution to community life. Finally, what women learned through
the training and literacy programs enhanced their self-esteem, with positive repercussions on the role they play in the
household and the village.43

 49.      Infrastructure construction and rehabilitation resulting from the sub-project component contributed to
 improved villages’ resilience through the creation of key community assets . As highlighted in the Efficacy Section,
 Component followed a CDD approach, as sub-projects to be implemented were chosen by the community based on the
 needs expressed by its members.47 Through the community sub-projects, several community assets were created (water
 reservoirs, community plantations, rehabilitation/creation of rural roads, fish breeding ponds, etc.), and anecdotal
 evidence collected during the ICR mission confirmed the relevance of these sub-projects in enhancing livelihood
 opportunities: e.g., water reservoirs allow for the cultivation of previously unused land, contributing to the availability of
 more vegetables, especially during the dry season, and providing water for livestock; community plantations allow for the
 cultivation of different vegetables, contributing to a more diverse diet, etc.

       Other Unintended Outcomes and Impacts

50.     The ICR mission observed that the Project triggered a positive dynamic within some communities by
strengthening social cohesion. Anecdotal evidence suggested that participation in sub-projects as well as training sessions
created regular interactions among beneficiaries, who now feel less isolated and perceive a sense of solidarity within the
community. This is particularly relevant for female beneficiaries, who traditionally had less opportunities for interactions
outside their household. Often, the joint work of two villages in one single project had the positive effect of creating
interactions and links between the two participating communities. These observations are confirmed in the final
evaluation, as surveyed village/village development committee chiefs claimed that the overall project implementation
approach triggered: (i) strengthened community union; (ii) enhanced social cohesion in the village; (iii) strengthened
community solidarity; (iv) a reorganization in the management of the village with regard to the organization of meetings
and the management of conflicts; and (v) a reduction in the rural exodus.

51.      In addition to the other impacts mentioned above, the creation of community infrastructure under Component
1, such as water reservoirs or reforestation, contributes to building climate vulnerability. Togo is increasingly prone to
climate hazards linked to floods, water scarcity, extreme heat, changes in precipitation patterns, violent winds, soil
erosion, landslides, among others48. These shocks tend to disproportionately affect the poorest, who are concentrated in
rural and dry areas and work in agriculture, and who have fewer means to adapt. The maintenance of community
infrastructures such as water reservoirs, for instance, may offer opportunities for continuing agricultural work in
prolonged dry seasons, allowing households to avoid coping mechanisms such as selling productive assets, skipping meals,
reducing access to healthcare and education, or traveling to neighboring countries to find employment opportunities
(thus depleting communities from their youth population). This outcome was particularly visible in the Savanes Region,
in the northern part of the country, which also is the poorest region with the highest exposure to extreme climatic hazards
such as prolonged dry seasons.

III. KEY FACTORS THAT AFFECTED IMPLEMENTATION AND OUTCOME

  A. KEY FACTORS DURING PREPARATION
52.      The Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project built on lessons learnt from
previous interventions in the country and international experiences on youth employment programs .49 The
PDCplus (P127200)50, implemented in Togo, showed the complementarity between the “hard” financing of
infrastructure and IGA, and the “soft” TA measures that trained communities and people.51 A 2012 evaluation
of World Bank support for youth employment prepared by the World Bank’s Independent Evaluation Group
(IEG) found strong evidence that programs that integrate multiple interventions are more likely to succeed
because they are better able to respond to the different needs of and different constraints faced by
beneficiaries. Along these lines, the Project adopted a holistic approach to address constraints that
beneficiaries are likely to face in multiple areas, such as, lack of soft/behavioral skills, money management
skills, marketing/planning, mentoring, and access to capital. In addition, the Project focus on community
service sub-projects as an entry point for youth employment was supported by the experience of interventions
such as the Sierra Leone Youth Employment Support Project52 and the Cote d’Ivoire Youth Employment and
Skills Development Project53, which used labor-intensive public works in a similar fashion, though with less
emphasis on On-the-Job Training. The provision of grants to address capital constraints was also supported by
evidence from youth employment projects in sub-Saharan Africa, most notably the Youth Opportunities
Project in Northern Uganda, which showed an impact on its participants in terms of increased business assets,
hours of work and earnings compared to the control group four years after the intervention.54

53.      The Project had well-structured components, with a clear operational logic linking activities to the
expected results, as shown within the retro-constructed ToC. The sequencing of activities and their content
carefully considered the local context: the timing for implementing sub-projects had originally been defined
to take into account the rotation between the harvest and the lean seasons (even though delays in
implementation hindered this timing), while the choice of sub-projects would depend on the predominant
economic activities in each sub-project location. The Project’s result framework is appropriate and
straightforward, as evidenced by the fact that indicators did not change during restructuring, with the only
exception of the reformulation of one PDO indicator to reflect the actual targeting method used by the Project
(please see Table 2 for details).

54.     The duration of the Project could have been considered more carefully at preparation. The Project
was initially designed to be implemented in three years based on a key assumption that did not materialize.
The original idea was to rely on the same staff who had worked within the PDCplus PIU (the Ministry of
Community Development, Handicraft, Youth and Employment), which would have saved time linked to hiring
of new staff and capacity building activities. Yet, following the successful implementation of two consecutive
IDA-funded projects (PDC and PDCplus), the Ministry of Community Development, Artisanship, Youth, and
Youth Employment (MDBAJEJ) was deemed to have developed the required experience and ANADEB was
designated as responsible for executing the Project within the Ministry and under its supervision. To address

 B. KEY FACTORS DURING IMPLEMENTATION
55.     Delays on key procurement activities impacted the preparation work and, consequently, the
effective start of implementation, as explained below. The Project was approved on March 21, 2017, and
became effective on November 30, 2017.

•        The procurement of a firm to conduct the study on existing employment opportunities in the 31
prefectures targeted by the project (covering the 150 cantons targeted by the interventions with an emphasis
on the identification of areas such as salaried jobs and self-employment offering employment opportunities
in the local labor market – agriculture and other rural trades) took five months to be approved instead of two
months as foreseen. Such undue delays were corrected during the implementation of the Project, as the CMU
and Government committed to shorten contract processing time, following a country portfolio performance
review (CPPR).

•       The Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) between ANADEB and the GIZ for the delivery of like skills
and micro-entrepreneurship skills to youth (the FoDA training package, which GIZ had developed in
partnership with the Government) took longer than expected to be signed between the two entities. This was
mainly due to lengthy negotiations between the two parties.

•       Due to two complaints about the recruitment of project staff, the recruitment for key positions in the
project was delayed by a few months. The complaints were related to the recruitment of grassroots
management training specialists who were critical staff for the implementation of the project. As required by
procurement guidelines, those two complaints had to be resolved before continuing implementation.

•        Some delay was reported in carrying out the environmental and social screening as part of the
validation of the community sub-projects, due to the limited number of environmental and social safeguards
specialists within the PIU. This was resolved eventually with the recruitment of junior environmental and social
consultants in all five regions, along with additional consultants to support the two safeguards specialists at
ANADEB headquarters.

•        Difficulties were reported in properly implementing FoDA training in certain regions, due to the lack
of qualified trainers. Given the target group of the project, mostly made up of youth who were not literate or
did not complete primary school, the FoDA training was supposed to be delivered in the local language to be
effective. This appeared to be a challenge in certain regions.

•       Delays in payments caused an initial lack of trust by beneficiaries towards the Project (widely reported
during the field visits); the issue was linked to the initial advance ceiling established in the Project’s designated
account which was not enough to keep up with the implementation pace hence with the amount needed to

57.      As a result, progress towards achieving the development objective was rated as moderately

achieved its development objective.56 The extension of the closing date until December 31, 2021 allowed the
necessary time to offer beneficiaries a complete support package (six months coaching, a one-month job

megaphones to coordinate beneficiaries. In addition, recognizing the impact that COVID-19 had on

amount, see the Efficiency section for more details on this).

IV. BANK PERFORMANCE, COMPLIANCE ISSUES, AND RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

 A. QUALITY OF MONITORING AND EVALUATION (M&E)

      M&E Design

60.     The PAD included relevant indicators linked to the PDO and the intermediate results. Indicators had clear data
source and target and baseline values. Only one intermediate result indicator was deemed as not explained enough within
the PAD at Project closing (refer to Table 5 for more details). Given that the Project would be the first of its kind specifically
focused on youth employment, with the implementation entrusted to a government agency with no prior Bank project
experience, indicators targets were prudently set at a level that was considered realistic at Project preparation stage. As
shown in the ToC, subcomponent 3.1 on capacity building for the Government to design and implement youth
employment programs was not linked to any indicator, yet results achieved in terms of strengthening the capacity of the
PIU can be taken as a proxy indicator for this activity.

61.    The PIU was primarily responsible for regular monitoring of activities and outputs, supported by the World
Bank Team. The M&E methods and tools identified within the PAD were: a) a computerized monitoring system to track

all outcome and intermediate indicators as well as other information required for effective project management; b)

62.     In addition, one of the key design features of the Project was based on meta-analysis about the relevance of
follow up systems in determining program performance. The Project included behavioral commitment devices to ensure
continuous follow up on beneficiaries: their participation to community sub-projects and the savings on sub-project
stipend allowed beneficiaries to participate in component two; their savings were constantly monitored through regular
checkups with partner microfinance institutions, and they had to complete the FODA modules successfully and submit a
basic business plan assessed as adequate by a coach. Following up on beneficiaries was not only necessary to assess
whether a given intervention was delivering the expected results, but also to promptly perform adjustments to the
intervention as required, both in the composition and intensity of different services.

   M&E Implementation

63.     Throughout implementation, various M&E tools provided information for monitoring the Project’s progress,
and there was systematic updating of the Project’s RF through supervision missions and regular video conferences.
Regular data collection and reporting were mainly coordinated and conducted by the PIU, which undertook
implementation support and monitoring missions to the beneficiary communities. As foreseen, an MIS was put in place
from the launch of the project capturing data on main characteristics of beneficiaries (gender, age, employment status)
and these data were updated on a continuous basis to monitor participation in the various activities of beneficiaries,
payments as well as results (including data on income of launched IGAs after six months of coaching). The structure of
the MIS, which would draw from the regional systems to inform the central one, posed at times some challenges in terms
of data consistency between the different regions as well as timely update. A technical consultant was hired to ensure
the effective functioning and regular updating of the MIS.

The project in fact applied a random selection of beneficiaries (please see Table 2 for more details on targeting) after

subscribing for participation to the Project in many communities, making it difficult to create a control group. A final
evaluation was carried out including 20 percent of the 200 beneficiary villages, randomly selected, where 560 young
beneficiaries were surveyed individually using a mobile application. The evaluation included individual/focus group
meetings with community development committees, intermediary NGOs, M&E firms/NGOs and coaches.

 B. ENVIRONMENTAL, SOCIAL, AND FIDUCIARY COMPLIANCE

68.       Environmental and social safeguards.

•        The Project was categorized under environmental category B because it had small scale and site-
specific environmental impacts, and, at closing, its performance on the application of the recommended social
and environmental safeguard measures was judged as Satisfactory. Community service sub-projects were
expected to involve creation or improvement of priority community infrastructure including feeder road
rehabilitation and maintenance, community agricultural projects and environmental management, which
triggered manageable small-scale and site-specific environmental impacts. The project developed an
Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF), adapted from that of the PDCplus project
(P127200), which was disclosed in-country and by the World Bank, and provided guidance on the screening
process for implementing community sub-projects.

•        OP 4.12 (Involuntary Resettlement) was triggered. Given that the exact location and type of sub-
project that would be selected by the beneficiary communities were not known at the start of implementation
phase, the Project prepared and disclosed a Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) based on the experience of
the PDCplus (P127200), which included similar activities. The RPF includes templates for sub-project screening
to identify any adverse social impacts and guided the preparation of subsequent Resettlement Action Plans
(RAPs)61 as and when necessary. Many of the sub-projects required land acquisition, which was done indeed

•        The Project developed and implemented a grievance and redress mechanism (GRM) which comprised

69.      Key social and environmental issues during implementation:

•        The Project encountered some difficulties in hiring and retaining safeguards specialists, as the first
two specialists hired resigned after barely one year on the job. It took several months to ANADEB to replace
them. Because of that, the PIU could not properly assess the technical quality of all community sub-projects.
This was pointed out in the final environmental and social audit report. The two safeguards specialists hired
by ANADEB were not able to cover all the 200 villages benefitting from sub-projects, hence junior
environmental and social safeguards specialists were hired to support monitoring of sub-projects in each of
the five regions. Only two of the newly hired junior specialists were able to attend the required training (in the
Maritime region), as the senior specialists in safeguards, responsible for the training, were engaged in the
preparation of the Additional Financing. This delay was also accentuated by the resignation of the ANADEB
social specialist, which led to a higher workload for the environmental specialist. Moreover, the
recommendation to hire junior specialists for 12 months was not fully followed, as they were hired for 6
months, without justification to the Bank team, with consequent difficulty in following up on sub-projects over
time and ensuring their quality. The lockdown imposed by the pandemic made field visits even more
complicated. Some issues relating to the process were noted: under pressure from its management to sped
up implementation, activities of some sub-projects began before screening reports were submitted to the
World Bank (upon request), and activities of Efalo Kope and Kominide sub-projects started before their RAP
and ESIA were finalized and approved.

•       Issues emerged with regards to the maintenance of community sub-projects, such as agricultural fields
or feeder roads. During the ICR mission field visit (for instance in the Sokodé area), beneficiaries reported that
in some villages non-beneficiaries showed some resistance towards collaborating into maintaining assets as
they had not benefitted from the Project. In other instances, despite the training received on maintenance of

 C. BANK PERFORMANCE

        Quality at Entry
     72.      The Project design was straightforward and strategically relevant. It drew upon solid evidence of prior
     analytical work and lessons learned from other youth employment World Bank-financed projects on the relevance of
     integrating multiple interventions in a way to respond to the constraints faced by beneficiaries in different areas, from
     soft behavioral to more technical skills as well as access to capital. It was also designed in a way to maintain
     beneficiaries’ continuous engagement to avoid dropouts, based on evidence linked to the importance of providing
     incentives for beneficiaries to stay in the program, as done through the Project’s savings component of the community
     service sub-projects. As shown within the ToC, Project activities were linked to outputs and outcomes in a
     straightforward way, while implementation arrangements built on previous experience within the country and
     carefully considered the local context. This includes, among other things, the focus on financing individual IGAs, as
     opposed to group IGAs (which was the approach under the PDCplus project) where final evaluation revealed that
     several of those groups were fictitious.

     73.      Nonetheless, three shortfalls in the design of the Project can be highlighted: (a) the Project did not foresee
     the lack of identification documents for a number of beneficiaries, which delayed implementation in the first phase,
     causing the need to undertake a series of initiatives in response (see Key Factors During Implementation section); (b)
     the Project did not foresee that migration of youth to urban areas and neighboring countries would represent an issue
     in many villages and, in turn, would affect the establishment of a baseline for the impact evaluation, which hindered
     the implementation of one of the key M&E tools foreseen within the PAD (please see M&E Implementation section);
     (c) the Project did not consider the burden of administrative and transportation costs to use the financial services
     offered by the Decentralized Financial System would impose on beneficiaries, with consequent need to undertake
     corrective measures (see Key Factors During Implementation section).

     74.     The choice of ANADEB as the PIU allowed for benefitting from synergies with the twinning IDA-supported
     Safety Nets and Basic Services Project (SNBSP) (P157038). ANADEB had already developed experience in managing
     labor-intensive public works projects modeled after those under PDC and PDCplus, and was responsible for
     implementing the SNBSP, with which this Project shared the grassroot management training approach and
     methodology for strengthening the capacities of targeted of beneficiary communities. Implementation of the project
     by ANADEB therefore ensured coherence and coordination with the SNBSP as well as synergies of technical staff
     responsible for the two projects.

     75.     The Project’s preparation was well coordinated with the Government. The team helped to design an
     intervention that could respond to the GoT needs as well as its priority to improve employment opportunities for the
     Togolese youth. The Project design was also well aligned with the Bank’s engagement within the country aiming to
     maximize skills-building and employment opportunities with a focus on youth and women.

    Quality of Supervision

76.      Quality of supervision was Highly Satisfactory. The Project benefitted from attentive and sustained
supervision from the World Bank team. Key Bank staff was based in the country, and proved always available to
support the PIU and, more broadly, to supervise implementation. The team held regular supervision missions in line
with World Bank guidelines, and, during the COVID-19 lockdown, these took place virtually to avoid disruption. The
supervision team produced nine ISRs. The reporting was candid, transparent, detailed, and informed relevant
adjustments to implementation. In 2019, for instance, the Project’s governance structure was adjusted at the level of
the PIU to give more responsibilities to the Project Coordinator and decentralize the decision-making process to
ensure more efficient day-to-day implementation. This proved to improve the pace of implementation of Project
activities. When delays in procurement and hiring of staff in the first year slowed implementation, the World Bank
team decided to institutionalize bi-monthly meetings with the PIU to discuss and accelerate implementation and to
put in place mitigation measures in a timely manner.

77.     The World Bank’s implementation support benefited from continuity in task management. The World Bank
Task Team Leaders (and Co-TTLs) and core members remained the same from the Project preparation until its
completion. This continuity was helpful in providing consistent and effective implementation support. The CMU was
involved from the Project design to its closing, and the team benefitted from quality control provided by the Africa
SPJ Global Practice-quality team and the CMU throughout the project cycle.


    Justification of Overall Rating of Bank Performance

78.      The overall rating of Bank performance is Satisfactory. The Project was well prepared despite a few
shortcomings in the design. The World Bank team was extremely proactive during implementation, supporting the
client in achieving the Project objectives, and reacting promptly to adjust to implementation changes as needed.


 D. RISK TO DEVELOPMENT OUTCOME

79.     The Risk to Development Outcome is Low in consideration of different elements, linked to the
Project achievements per se, the government support as well as the role of the Bank in future initiatives:

•       The Project yielded positive effects in terms of youth employment and employability by offering
beneficiaries training and coaching on business development (e.g., how to prepare a viable business plan) and
by supporting them in the launch or consolidation of income-generating opportunities which can be sustained
over time. By giving them access to financial institutions and by establishing a culture of savings (including the
creation of saving groups in many villages), the Project provided beneficiaries with key instruments towards
youth entrepreneurship which will remain after its closure. In addition, the ongoing institutionalization of
FODA at the national level will promote the acquisition of business-development skills among the youth.

•       The Project generated positive externalities for participating communities through the creation of
assets that are likely to increase villages’ livelihood opportunities and that can be maintained over time with
careful management arrangements and by drawing from the grassroots management training curriculum
provided by the Project.

•      The PIU prepared an exit strategy that was discussed with local governments and other stakeholders
and reached some level of arrangements on how to sustain community assets and communities as well as


                                                                                                               Page 30 of 65
       The World Bank
      Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project (P157036)



individual IGAs. The strategy foresees that single communities will ensure maintenance of the sub-projects
realized within their territorial jurisdiction and NGOs/voluntary associations will keep following and supporting
the IGAs launched in their area of intervention under ANADEB supervision. The materials and equipment used
to realize the sub-projects will be returned to the village development committees for the management of
assets. The deployment of the necessary resources for the full financing of the exit strategy is still under
discussion. Beyond the government, ANADEB has approached GIZ to mobilize additional resources to support
the implementation of the exit strategy. Discussions on this front are still ongoing.

•        The Ministry of Grassroots Development, Youth, and Youth Employment is discussing the
institutionalization of the Project into a national youth employment program, which would allow for the
capitalization of Project achievements. This program could be structured as a national rural youth initiative on
a large scale and/or be linked to other initiatives such as the Youth Economic Initiative Support Fund (Fonds
d’Appui aux Initiatives Économiques des Jeunes, FAIEJ) and the Support Project for Youth Employability in the
Key Sectors (Projet d’Appui à l’Employabilité et à l’Insertion des Jeunes dans les Secteurs Porteurs, PAEIJ-SP).
The establishment of a governmental youth employment program targeting rural areas nation-wide would
not only be relevant for tackling rural under- and unemployment but could be particularly critical considering
the increasing spread of jihadist ideologies among segments of the youth population (and the consequent rise
in violence) in the North of the country, across the areas bordering Burkina Faso, and is therefore being
attentively discussed within the GoT.

•        Results and insights from the Project may inform the reform of policies and regulations in youth
employment and job creation in Togo. As a result of the collaboration initiated through the Project between
GiZ, ANADEB/the GoT and the World Bank, a number of ASAs have been launched through EFO-funding from
GiZ. First, based on a review of national strategies, a study to identify value chains with a high potential for
better jobs outcomes for youth and women in Togo has been conducted. Second, in 2021 a multisectoral Jobs
Diagnostic looking at constraints to and opportunities for job creation on the macro-, labor demand- and
supply side was launched. Through regular dialogue with key actors of the jobs agenda in Togo, the Jobs
Diagnostic is aligned with the second strategic axis of the Roadmap for Togo 2025 ( Feuille de Route
Gouvernementale 2025) and will distill policy recommendations for the GoT’s Jobs Strategy.64

•        On June 23, 2021, the Bank approved an Additional Financing (AF)65 to the Togo Safety Nets and Basic
Services Project (Projet Filets Sociaux et Services de Base, FSB; P157038), which includes an economic inclusion
component based on the design and experience of the Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth
Project. While the AF includes a COVID-19 response objective, Component 2 of the AF will offer households a
package of economic inclusion support activities. The Project’s design in combining and sequencing of the
training with a business grant and coaching, as well as the FODA modules piloted during the intervention have
served as a blueprint for the AF’s Component 2.66

•        Programs that integrate both the demand and the supply side prove effective in promoting youth
employment. To facilitate the integration of youth into the labor market, interventions may combine the
provision of skills to youth (i.e., the supply side of the labor market) with efforts to ensure matching labor
demand, e.g., through opportunities for self-employment. While stand-alone supply side interventions lead to
mixed results,67 the experience of the Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project shows
that integrating supply and demand components in the same intervention yields positive outcomes. Also,
initiatives that integrate multiple interventions (e.g., combining skills with access to capital and to financial
institutions) are more likely to have an impact on youth employment and entrepreneurship because they are
better able to respond to a range of constraints faced by participants. In the experience of the Togo
Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project, the sequencing of training, coaching for the
preparation of a viable business plan and provision of funding, coupled with the opening of bank accounts,
proved appropriate to address beneficiaries’ lack of skills and options for financial inclusion.

•        An intervention that promotes savings alongside the enhancement of skills not only allows
participants to start or expand an entrepreneurial activity, but it is likely to enhance its sustainability over
time by facilitating re-investments. Saving promotion can be fostered by assuring that enough man-days are
provided in community sub-projects/public work components to allow beneficiaries to accumulate a part of
their stipend and by providing them with financial education. Within the Togo Employment Opportunities for
Vulnerable Youth Project, this approach was accompanied by the requirement of having saved a certain
amount (i.e., a minimum of FCFA 40,000) to receive the matching grant. In addition, the promotion of saving
can be enhanced through the opening of bank accounts or the establishment of saving groups in villages
located far from financial institutions, as done within the Project. According to ANADEB’s implementation
report, in 2021, in eight months FCFA 47,000,750 were mobilized by the youth in the form of savings in the 57
village where saving groups were supported by ANADEB under the Project: FCFA 19,005,000 in outstanding
loans and FCFA 6,587,225 in solidarity funds.68 Participants in the schemes in each village have thus generated
the resources they need to reinvest in their income generating activities.

•        The provision of functional literacy within business-development training programs is critical in
contexts of widespread analphabetism. In the Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project,
and on the initiative of ANADEB, simplification of literacy and basic numeracy skills were offered as preliminary
modules in preparation for micro-entrepreneurial training.69 Anecdotal evidence collected during the ICR
mission confirmed the relevance of this approach in enabling participants with no schooling to keep simplified
accounts and develop basic business plans. Testing of literacy and basic numeracy skills may be appropriate in
similar contexts (i.e., vis-à-vis the targeted population and the specific country context). Beneficiaries can then
be grouped accordingly, and those lacking the needed skills can be offered literacy and numeracy skills training
as a pre-requisite for accessing business development/entrepreneurship training.

Project and the (implementation) capacities developed within ANADEB, the intervention should be

    81.      Considerations with respect to program design and implementation:

    •       Responsive task management and attentive supervision can rapidly address emerging contextual
    issues by adapting coordination and implementation arrangements. During the implementation of the Togo
    Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project, the decision-making process was
    revised/decentralized to ensure timely mitigation measures when required and a prompt restructuring was
    proposed to address delays in implementation. In parallel, implementation arrangements were adjusted to
    address field-level realities and unforeseeable challenges such as lockdown imposed by the COVID-19
    pandemic. These adjustments were made possible by sustained in-country supervision and regular,
    transparent reporting. Also, sustained support to the PIU mitigated risks associated with poor management of
    (procurement, financial management, implementation) procedures and processes required for managing a
    Bank-funded project. This type of support could include specific agreements to clarify roles and
    responsibilities, capacity-building sessions and mentoring for staff, regular supervision on the Unit’s activity
    and other initiatives such as PIU participation to learning events or exchanges with other countries. In the
    Togo Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project, these approaches were successful in enhancing
    implementation capacity of the PIU and avoiding procurement issues.

    •        Implementation strategies that rely on synergies with available actors allow to save resources in
    terms of time and funding. Within the Project, the choice of ANADEB as the implementing agency allowed for
    benefitting of a similar approach and coordination with the twinning SNBSP as well as interactions of technical
    staff responsible for the two projects, leading to lower administrative costs. The partnership with GIZ allowed
    for the prompt roll out of life and micro-entrepreneurship skills training to youth (the FoDA training package),
    which had already been developed by GIZ in cooperation with the GoT when project implementation started.

                                                                   ANNEX 4. EFFICIENCY ANALYSIS

Introduction

Beyond the discussion of efficacy, the efficiency analysis this section considers the resources invested relative to
the project’s outcomes. This is primarily measured via cost-benefit analysis (CBA) that yields estimates for the
social rate of return (SRR) of the project. CBA is also used to show, quantitatively, how the project design, by
pairing a social safety net (i.e., LIPWs) and an entrepreneurship support intervention led to greater efficiency
than had they been implemented as separate interventions. It is worth noting that there was no ex-ante
economic analysis of the project’s impacts due to lack of reliable data and evidence, and therefore no
comparison with earlier assessments of returns to the project.71

Main Analysis Framework

The economic analysis is predicated on the change in income of project beneficiaries and broader economy. The
project SRR is defined as the rate of return that sets the net present value (NPV) of the project to zero. We
divide the benefits into those associated with the LIPWs and those with the entrepreneurship support activities.
The costs associated with training, subcomponent 1.2, is split evenly between LIPWs and entrepreneurship
activities since training was part of the support necessary for success of entrepreneurship aspects as well as the
basis for stipend payments during the LIPW phase of the project. The benefits counted for the LIPW activities
are based on the immediate expenditures (portion of the LIPW earnings that were not saved by beneficiaries)
and a portion of the operations costs of the community project that did not go to labor/beneficiary payments
(i.e., inputs and management costs). The benefits from the entrepreneurship support activities include the
future income gains from IGA as well as two transfers that were made during program implementation.72 For
both types of income effects, an approximation of the foregone income (counterfactual) is subtracted to account
for opportunity costs, as discussed further below. With this specification, the NPV equation that defines the SRR
is:

Assessment of program design: measuring complementarity between project components

This project pairs two interventions that typically serve different purposes in such a way as to increase their
individual efficiency (vis-à-vis to implementing them separately). The LIPW activities serve the role of a social
safety net to enable individuals to cope with income disruptions and other shocks. Entrepreneurship support is
aimed to enable more lucrative IGAs, and better long-term income prospects. While LIPWs are expected to have
welfare benefits, only a portion can be quantified; and since they are meant for smoothing consumption against
income shocks, it is unsurprising that impact evaluations have had mixed results regarding their ability to have
long-term income effects.75 Some mechanisms through which a LIPW scheme would generate long-term effects
are seldom measured – such as the negative long-term effects of distressed asset sales to cope with shocks or
the local economy benefits derived from the public work’s output (e.g., drainage infrastructure, dam
rehabilitation). The most tractable benefits are local income multipliers- the effects on increased local
consumption by beneficiaries. A study assessing the income multiplier from a cash transfer program in Ethiopia76
applies a local economy-wide simulation and finds the income multiplier to be around 1.577; each dollar spent
generates 1.5 dollars of income for the community. On the other hand, interventions supporting
entrepreneurship and higher-paying IGAs, as would be expected, have a better track record in terms of long-
term effects.78

There are two main channels considered through which combining components led to improved outcomes: (1)
incentivizing beneficiaries to only spend what is needed from their LIPWs payments and (2) having LIPWs act as
a targeting mechanism for beneficiaries who end up joining the entrepreneurship support component. The first
channel refers to using matching grants as incentive to make individuals assess how much they need to
satisfactorily cope with consumption shocks. This avoids ‘overshooting’ on the support needed through the
LIPWs to satisfactorily cope with shocks. Thus, the pairing of the interventions causes beneficiaries to organically
shift program resources from the lower return LIPW activities to the higher return entrepreneurship support
activities. Having the savings from the LIPW earnings act as seed capital in a matching grants scheme leads to an
increase in the number of individuals that can be supported by entrepreneurship support activities (assuming
the same total grant amount per individual). The second channel considered is the beneficiary targeting enabled

Estimating the Pproject’s net effects on future earnings

Given an impact evaluation could not be conducted as planned due to a lack of oversubscription from
beneficiaries, we consider multiple ‘without program’ income scenarios (i.e., counterfactuals) for beneficiaries
to calculate income gains attributable to the program. These counterfactuals can be interpreted as the
beneficiary opportunity cost of labor. For immediate expenditures (∆������������������������������������������������������������,������ ) associated with the LIPW, we
use the observed savings rate and consider what they would have consumed out of the total income they would
have generated by applying a counterfactual wage rate to the time devoted to the LIPW activities. To measure
the net effect on future earnings from entrepreneurship support activities (∆������   ������������������������������������ ������������������,������ ) we take the difference
between what beneficiaries reported earning six months after the start-ups launched and the projected monthly
earnings associated with each counterfactual scenario.

Three counterfactual scenarios are considered (Table A4.2). Scenario 1, the least conservative case, uses the
monthly earnings reported by beneficiaries at the beginning of activities as their counterfactual wage. At that
point, 42 percent of beneficiaries reported having income and the (unconditional) mean monthly income

Results

The large net effects on future incomes from the entrepreneurship support and benefits of linking LIPW and
entrepreneurship through using beneficiary savings resulted to a significant return to the project between 53
and 88 percent, with a middle scenario of 72 percent (Table A4.3). As expected, and consistent with the
literature, the measurable84 long-term benefits from the LIPW were small, especially since the project funds turn
into consumption in the present and no long-term effects are accounted for. The efficiency of the LIPW is on par
with what have found elsewhere – for example an earlier study85 finds that on average it takes US$3.5 to
generate US$1 of income benefits as compared to a range between US$3.30, and US$4.40, and US$5.72.86 The
entrepreneurship Component, because of the significant income effect, has a sizeable rate of return of 128
percent with 97 and 150 percent for the most and least conservative counterfactuals, respectively.

The results show that the increased support from having beneficiaries use savings as seed capital due to
combining the entrepreneurship support/training with LIPWs yielded an increase of 30 to 40 percent greater
return as compared to separately (Table A4.3). The likely greater net effect on future income because of the
targeting that LIPW afforded is not accounted for in this comparison (i.e., same net income effect applied to
benefits calculation of component implemented separately). Because the analysis of LIPW being implemented
separately assumes the full program wages consumed, as compared to only the portion the was not saved in the
case of the combined implementation, is manifested in a slightly lower LIPW support ROI- from 23 to 36 percent
in the middle scenario. The decrease in the LIPW is more than made up by the increase in the return from the
entrepreneurship component, which went from 75 to 128 percent in the middle scenario. Assuming everyone
received the same size grant regardless of implemented separately or together, the calculation suggests that
implemented separately the entrepreneurship component would have supported 6,963 beneficiaries rather
than 11,495 when implemented together.

Box A7.1. Highlights from the ICR mission women’s focus groups

Did you have a say in the choice of the sub-project to be implemented in the village?
In all villages, women agree they were invited to the meeting at the level of the village development committees
to select together the types of projects the community could benefit from, based on a list of works proposed by
the Project on the basis of the needs of the community and selected according to the predominant economic
activity in the area. The actual sub-project was then selected through a draw of sort.
How was your work within the sub-project, especially vis-à-vis the work that men would perform?
Women agree they were assigned tasks appropriate to their strength and not heavier than those assigned to
men.
Were you able to participate regularly in activities in the sub-projects? Did your participation create issues
within the household?
Women agree they were able to participate on a regular basis and their participation did not raise any issue within the
household.
Are you satisfied with the realized sub-projects?
Women agree sub-projects offered new livelihood opportunities as well as opportunities to start income-generating
activities within the village. Women report they want to maintain the assets created in their villages after the end of
the Project.
Were you able to attend the FODA training and did you find it helpful? If so, in which way?
Most interviewed women were able to attend the FODA training. All attendees agree that it was very useful to learn
basic things they had never thought of before. For instance, women learnt that they have to wear clean clothes when
they go to the market to sell their products, or that they have to keep their small shop tidy if they want client to enter
and buy. Also, some report they learnt they must wash their hands before preparing and selling food, etc.
Was literacy training useful?

     The World Bank
    Employment Opportunities for Vulnerable Youth Project (P157036)



Many women report that, after the literacy training, they are able to perform basic operations such as writing their
name or doing simple calculations. Beyond being useful for their subsequent participation to business-development
training, this had a positive impact on their self-esteem. Thanks to the acquired literacy skills and after completing the
FODA training and the coaching, some women are able to keep simplified accounts and develop basic business plans.
Not all women report the same degree of assimilation of literacy. For some, the duration of the courses was
insufficient for them to be able to progress or benefit from it.
How was your experience with coaching for business development?
Coaching was useful to participant women and many of them were able to prepare a business plan for the
establishment of an IGA taking into account actual market demand locally. Coaches would regularly visit villages
during the roll out of the six-month mentoring, but many participants complain that they stopped being available at
the end of the mentoring period, while women would have benefitted from a support sustained over a longer time.
Were you able to establish and maintain an IGA with the support provided by the Project?
Many women were able to establish an IGA, mainly food preparation or processing (e.g., soya cheese), production and
selling of small products (e.g., soap), creation of small village shops for re-selling products, raising of goats and
poultry.
Overall, how do you feel about your participation in the Project? Did it bring some changes beyond the
establishment of your IGA?
Thanks to the Project stipend, women are now able to provide for basic needs, such as ensuring food within the
household and paying for medicines. Their participation in the program also had positive repercussions for their self-
esteem, as women and as mothers: some report that with the money from the Project they were finally able to buy
soap for washing themselves and their clothes, while many report that they were finally able to provide for the needs
of their children. Participation led to enhanced self-confidence and increased respect within the household: thanks to
earnings made through the project, women tend to be more respected by men within the household, hence less
subject to violence at home; they are now perceived as breadwinners and less dependent on their husbands.
Participation to the Project also led to a more proactive role within the community: female participation during village
meetings to discuss sub-project activities strengthened their self-confidence vis-à-vis the rest of the community,
increasing their active contribution to community life.

              ANNEX 8. ADMINISTRATIVE STRUCTURE OF TOGO and AREAS TARGETED BY THE PROJECT

From an administrative point of view, Togo is divided into:

    •   5 regions: from north to south the regions are Savanes (with capital Dapaong), Kara (with capital
        Kara), Centrale (with capital Sokodé), Plateaux (with capital Atakpamé) and Maritime (with capital Lomé);

    •   39 prefectures;

    •   117 communes;

    •   394 cantons;

    •   3,644 villages.



The Project targeted 200 villages in the 150 poorest cantons of the country, located in 31 prefectures across the 5
regions, as follows:

    •   Savanes Region: 7 prefectures, 43 cantons and 57 villages.

    •   Kara Region: 7 prefectures, 33 cantons and 44 villages.

    •   Centrale Region: 5 prefectures, 25 cantons and 34 villages.

    •   Plateaux Region: 8 prefectures, 31 cantons and 41 villages.

    •   Maritime Region: 4 prefectures, 18 cantons and 24 villages.
